revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23989927232|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:手湯]]
{{Unreferenced|date=24 April 2014 (Thursday) 03:28 (UTC)}} [[File:Godzilla no teyu.JPG|thumb|300px|{{ill|Utoro Hot Spring|de|Utoro Onsen|ja|ウトロ温泉|zh|宇登呂温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utoro Hot Spring|1=Utoro Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11289024}} Godzilla's Hand Bath]]
'''Teyu''' (手湯) is a {{ill|Bathing|en|Bathing|qid=Q327651|lt=Bathing|1=Bathing}} method where one immerses their {{ill|Hand|en|Hand|qid=Q33767|lt=Hand|1=Hand}}s into a {{ill|Hot spring|en|Hot spring|qid=Q177380|lt=Hot spring|1=Hot spring}} or similar source. It also refers to the facility or place where such a method is available. '''Shuyoku''' or '''Teyoku''' (手浴) refers to the same method and appears in caregiver certification exams. It is studied in the field of health sciences.
It is a facility that allows an even more casual experience of hot springs compared to {{ill|Ashiyu|en|Ashiyu|qid=Q725522|lt=ashiyu|1=Ashiyu}}. Unlike footbaths, teyus do not necessarily include seating, which typically results in shorter immersion times. There are two types of hand immersion: one where the hand is dipped into a basin and another where the hand is held under a hot spring spout. Sometimes the basin-type is installed together with a footbath.
It is believed that the first teyu was installed at {{ill|Lake Akan Hot Springs|de|Akan-ko Onsen|ja|阿寒湖温泉|zh|阿寒湖温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lake Akan Hot Springs|1=Akanko Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11657382}}<ref>[http://ja.kushiro-lakeakan.com/things_to_do/3652/ Kushiro / Lake Akan Official Tourist Site: Origin of Teyu at Lake Akan] Accessed 7 November 2017</ref>. Since then, teyus have spread as an accessible way to enjoy hot springs, and there are even hot spring areas like {{ill|Lake Tōya Hot Springs|de|Toyako Onsen|ja|洞爺湖温泉|zh|洞爺湖温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lake Tōya Hot Springs|1=Lake Toya Hot Spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11555858}} and {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|en|Dōgo Onsen|qid=Q1037748|lt=Dōgo Onsen|1=Dōgo Onsen}} where touring teyus and footbaths is a local specialty.
Teyoku may be applied to hospitalized patients who are unable to bathe or wash hands<ref name="review2020"/>. As of 2019, three randomized controlled trials and nine other studies had investigated teyoku. Water temperature ranged between 39-42°C with typical immersion times of 10 minutes. They observed increases in skin temperature in areas not immersed<ref name="review2020">{{Cite journal |language=ja |author=Keita Sugawara |date=2020 |title=Literature Review on the Effects of Teyoku |journal=Journal of the Japanese Society of Nursing Art and Science |volume=19 |issue=0 |pages=33-42 |doi=10.18892/jsnas.19.0_33 |url=https://doi.org/10.18892/jsnas.19.0_33}}</ref>. In winter, immersing the right hand for 10 minutes raised surface temperature and moisture content of the left hand for an hour after 15 minutes<ref>{{Cite journal |language=ja |authors=Ruriko Okada, Kanji Matsukawa, Toshio Kobayashi, Yukiko Miyakoshi |date=1 August 2013 |title=Moisturizing Effect of One-Sided Teyoku |journal=Taiikugaku Kenkyu (Journal of Physical Fitness) |volume=62 |issue=4 |pages=315-321 |doi=10.7600/jspfsm.62.315 |url=https://doi.org/10.7600/jspfsm.62.315}}</ref>. Compared with full-body bathing, teyoku demonstrated comparable effects on temperature perception in non-immersed body parts<ref>{{Cite journal |language=ja |authors=Harumi Yamaguchi et al. |date=2019 |title=Can Teyoku Substitute for Full-Body Bathing? |journal=Mukogawa Women's University Nursing Journal |volume=4 |pages=13-23 |doi=10.14993/00001418 |url=https://doi.org/10.14993/00001418}}</ref>. A relaxation effect was observed<ref>{{Cite journal |language=ja |authors=Mie Yoshida, Akiko Joukamo, Kimiko Akamatsu |date=2012 |title=Effects of Teyoku on Autonomic Nervous System and Pleasant Emotions in Women |journal=Journal of the Japan Academy of Nursing Research |volume=35 |issue=3 |pages=3_122 |doi=10.15065/jjsnr.20120607049 |url=https://doi.org/10.15065/jjsnr.20120607049}}</ref>. Teyoku helped regulate autonomic nervous balance by shifting the dominance between sympathetic and parasympathetic states<ref>{{Cite journal |language=ja |authors=Hajime Nakano, Etsurou Hori |date=2021 |title=Regulatory Effect of Teyoku on Autonomic Nervous System |journal=Journal of the Japanese Society of Nursing Art and Science |volume=20 |issue=0 |pages=11-19 |doi=10.18892/jsnas.20.0_11 |url=https://doi.org/10.18892/jsnas.20.0_11}}</ref>. A 15-person study on elderly participants showed that teyoku 2 hours before bedtime led to longer sleep and better fatigue recovery, although the effect on falling asleep varied widely<ref>{{Cite journal |language=ja |authors=Naomi Iwane et al. |date=2012 |title=Impact of Teyoku on Sleep in Elderly People |journal=Journal of the Japan Academy of Nursing Research |volume=35 |issue=3 |pages=3_120 |doi=10.15065/jjsnr.20120607045 |url=https://doi.org/10.15065/jjsnr.20120607045}}</ref>. Brain blood flow increased by an average of 12.4% in 10 adults and 28.2% in 6 hospitalized patients<ref>{{Cite journal |language=ja |authors=Hiromi Okita et al. |title=Changes in Brain Blood Flow from Teyoku Stimulation |journal=Japanese Red Cross Medical Journal |volume=8 |issue=1 |date=October 1998 |pages=151}}</ref>.
Though less effective, even immersing only the thumb ("thumb-yoku") produced some heat therapy benefits<ref>{{Cite journal |language=ja |authors=Tadashi Ooshige, Miko Awata |date=2012 |title=Effectiveness of Teyoku and Thumb-Yoku |journal=Japanese Journal of Physical Therapy Supplement |volume=2011 |pages=Db0545 |doi=10.14900/cjpt.2011.0.Db0545.0 |url=https://doi.org/10.14900/cjpt.2011.0.Db0545.0}}</ref>.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:手足湯.JPG|{{ill|Matsubarako Onsen|de|Matsubarako Onsen|ja|松原湖温泉|zh|松原湖温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsubarako Onsen|qq=}} Yatsumine no Yu (hand bath with {{ill|Ashiyu|en|Ashiyu|qid=Q725522|lt=ashiyu|1=Ashiyu}}) </gallery>
